child_summaries.title: Phase 4: Round-trip a one-window session skeleton
Phase 4: Restore panel topology and focus per workspace
Phase 4: Enforce launch restore policy and safe fallback
Phase 4: Capture and replay terminal scrollback safely
Phase 4: Persist typed browser panel state contract
Phase 4: Autosave quiet sessions and save synchronously on quit
Phase 4: Verify full-session behavior across save, quit, and relaunch

agent_notes: Further note from bead: Phase 4 should replace the minimal earlier persistence with a durable full-session model. The design should stay simple for a personal single-machine app, but the persistence boundary should be deep enough to test independently and protect against data loss.
content.context: Argus currently restores only enough state to reopen basic workspaces. Daily use needs relaunch to preserve tab focus, split layout, scrollback context, sidebar state, panel-specific state, and future browser panel state so users can continue without manual reconstruction.

decisions.text: Introduce a dedicated session persistence boundary that owns encoding, decoding, validation, save policy, restore policy, and file I/O rather than keeping full persistence embedded in workspace orchestration.
The session snapshot uses one schema version. No schema migration; version mismatch discards the snapshot and starts fresh.
The top-level snapshot models an array of windows for forward compatibility; v1 writes and accepts exactly one window.
Schema enforces a maximum of 12 windows and 128 workspaces per window; v1 runtime remains single-window.
Project snapshots include stable identifiers, names, repository paths, main branches, expand/collapse state, workspace identifiers, optional colors, and catch-all status.
Workspace snapshots include project association, worktree path, worktree branch, display title state, selected panel, ordered tabs, and panel layout state.

Panel snapshots are typed. Terminal panel snapshots capture surface identity, working directory, title metadata, and scrollback payload. Browser panel snapshots capture current URL, zoom level, and developer tools visibility.
Sidebar state is part of the application snapshot, including left sidebar visibility/width and right git sidebar visibility/width.
Terminal scrollback capture is encapsulated behind a small interface so truncation and ANSI-boundary handling can be tested independently from Ghostty rendering.
Terminal scrollback is capped at 400,000 characters per terminal panel.
When truncating scrollback, avoid starting replay inside a partial ANSI escape sequence and wrap truncated replay text in ANSI reset codes.
Autosave runs approximately every 8 seconds only after quiet periods, avoiding excessive writes while the user is actively typing or the terminal is producing high-volume output.
Quit save is synchronous and completes before app termination continues.
Restore is skipped when explicitly disabled by environment, when launched with file or URL arguments, or when running in automated test environments.
Ephemeral runtime state such as agent status entries, agent PIDs, notifications, transient loading state, and live process identity is not restored.

content.goal: Save and restore a complete versioned Argus session for the single main window without restoring unsafe or stale runtime state.
out_of_scope: Schema migrations between multiple persistence versions.
Multi-window runtime support; only the schema is forward-compatible.
Restoring live shell processes, process IDs, agent state, or command execution state.
Browser panel UI implementation beyond the persistence contract required for browser panel state.
Changing git status refresh behavior or worktree management semantics.
Replacing Ghostty configuration persistence; terminal settings continue to come from Ghostty-compatible config.
content.source: Migrated from bead argus-o2m (PRD: Phase 4 Session Persistence).

user_stories.text: As an Argus user, I want my projects and workspaces restored after relaunch, so that I can resume my workspace organization without rebuilding it.
As an Argus user, I want each workspace's tab order restored, so that my muscle memory for tabs remains intact.
As an Argus user, I want the active workspace and active tab restored, so that relaunch returns me to the work I was last doing.
As an Argus user, I want terminal panels to reopen in their last known directories, so that new shell sessions start in the right context.
As an Argus user, I want split-pane layouts restored, so that complex terminal arrangements survive restart.
As an Argus user, I want terminal scrollback restored up to a large limit, so that recent command output and agent logs remain available after restart.
As an Argus user, I want restored scrollback to preserve colors and avoid malformed terminal control bytes, so that restored terminal output is readable and safe.
As an Argus user, I want browser panels to remember their URL, zoom, and developer tools state, so that browser workflows can resume when browser panels exist.
As an Argus user, I want sidebar visibility and widths restored, so that the window layout matches my preferred setup.
As an Argus user, I want Argus to autosave during quiet periods, so that crashes or forced restarts lose little state.
As an Argus user, I want Argus to save synchronously when quitting, so that normal quit never races against session persistence.
As an Argus user, I want launch arguments to suppress restore, so that opening Argus for a file or URL does not replace that intent with an old session.
As an Argus user, I want an environment variable to disable restore, so that scripts and troubleshooting can force a clean session.
As a test runner, I want automated tests to skip session restore, so that tests are deterministic and isolated from user state.
As an Argus user, I want incompatible session files discarded rather than partially interpreted, so that schema changes do not corrupt the running session.
As an Argus user, I want empty or oversized snapshots rejected, so that corrupted data cannot create an unusable app state.
As an Argus user, I do not want stale agent statuses, process IDs, or other ephemeral runtime state restored, so that the UI does not show dead processes as active.

verification: Pure snapshot tests cover Codable round trips, schema version rejection, empty snapshot rejection, window/workspace caps, one-window enforcement, and project/workspace reconciliation.
Scrollback tests cover the 400,000-character cap, ANSI escape boundary truncation, reset wrapping, empty scrollback, plain text, and mixed control sequences.
Restore-policy tests cover environment-disabled restore, automated-test restore suppression, launch-argument restore suppression, incompatible snapshot fallback, and oversized snapshot fallback.
Autosave tests use controllable clocks/schedulers or test seams rather than wall-clock sleeps where possible.
Workspace restoration tests build on existing Phase 2 session, reconciliation, terminal directory, split shortcut, and tab-order tests.
Sidebar persistence tests build on existing sidebar state tests and verify both left and git sidebar state through the session snapshot.
Git status and agent-related tests confirm ephemeral status-like runtime state is absent from restored sessions when those systems exist.
content.what_to_build: A full-session persistence boundary that preserves projects, workspaces, ordered tabs, focused panel, split layouts, terminal working directories, bounded terminal scrollback, browser panel state, and sidebar visibility/width. Saves happen automatically during typing-quiet periods and synchronously on quit. Restores happen on launch only when safe and intended; incompatible, empty, oversized, test, argument-based, or explicitly disabled restores start fresh.

acceptance_criteria.text: Saving a session with projects, catch-all workspace membership, selected workspace, terminal tab count/directories, and left/right sidebar state produces a versioned snapshot with exactly one window.
Restoring that snapshot recreates the same user-visible project/workspace/sidebar state and selected workspace.
Incompatible schema versions, empty workspace lists, more than one v1 window, more than 12 schema windows, or more than 128 workspaces in the window are rejected by starting fresh.
The existing Phase 2 reconciliation behavior for project/workspace cross-references remains covered.
Tests cover Codable round trip, invalid snapshot fallback, and integration through WorkspaceManager rather than only isolated structs.

agent_notes: Blocked by: none — can start immediately.
Keep the PRD rule: one schema version, no migrations, v1 runtime single-window, schema cap 12 windows, 128 workspaces per window.

files.reason: Current minimal session snapshot model to replace/extend.
Current save/restore orchestration and integration point.
Left/right sidebar persisted state to bring under session snapshot.
Existing terminal directory snapshot prior art.

content.goal: Phase 4: Round-trip a one-window session skeleton
content.source: Migrated from bead argus-o2m.1; parent/source bead argus-o2m. Reference user stories: US1, US2, US3, US4, US9, US15, US16.
content.what_to_build: Replace the current minimal Phase 2 persistence path with a full-session skeleton that saves and restores one Argus window end to end. The slice should cover the top-level session envelope, one-window schema, project/workspace reconciliation, selected workspace restoration, basic terminal panel snapshots, and sidebar visibility/width in a durable SessionManager-style boundary. Existing app behavior should still start with a fresh catch-all workspace when no valid snapshot exists.

title: Phase 4: Round-trip a one-window session skeleton

acceptance_criteria.text: A workspace with multiple terminal tabs restores the same tab order and active tab.
A workspace with vertical and horizontal split panes restores the same pane tree and focused pane.
Closing/reopening from a snapshot preserves terminal panel working directories for both tabs and split panes.
Restored panel IDs and layout references are internally consistent; dangling layout leaves are discarded or cause a fresh safe fallback for that workspace.
Tests build on existing terminal tab ordinal and split shortcut tests and assert external workspace behavior, not private storage details.

agent_notes: Blocked by migrated issue for argus-o2m.1.

PanelLayoutNode is not currently Codable; add a durable snapshot representation rather than leaking implementation details if cleaner. Preserve ordinal tab labels and keep split panes out of top-level tabs.

files.reason: Contains panelOrder, panels, activePanelId, and tabLayouts.
Terminal panel state and surface identity.
Existing tab title behavior prior art.

content.goal: Phase 4: Restore panel topology and focus per workspace
content.source: Migrated from bead argus-o2m.2; parent/source bead argus-o2m. Reference user stories: US2, US3, US4, US5.
content.what_to_build: Extend the session skeleton so each workspace restores its complete terminal panel topology: ordered top-level tabs, selected/focused panel, active tab, split-pane layout tree, terminal working directories, and durable panel/surface identifiers where safe. The restored workspace should look and behave like the saved workspace before scrollback is considered.

title: Phase 4: Restore panel topology and focus per workspace

acceptance_criteria.text: ARGUS_DISABLE_SESSION_RESTORE=1 starts a fresh session even when a valid snapshot exists.
Automated test environments start fresh and do not read the user session file.
File or URL launch arguments suppress restore and start from the explicit launch intent instead of the old session.
Invalid JSON, incompatible schema, empty snapshots, oversized snapshots, and invalid window counts are handled by starting fresh.
Snapshot models and restore code do not include agent status entries, agent PIDs, notifications, live process identity, transient loading state, or other ephemeral runtime state.

agent_notes: Blocked by migrated issue for argus-o2m.1.

Expand WorkspaceManager.isRunningUnderAutomatedTests into an explicit launch restore policy that can be unit tested without real NSApplication process state. No schema migration: mismatched schema version discards the whole snapshot.

files.reason: Current test/env restore suppression lives here.

content.goal: Phase 4: Enforce launch restore policy and safe fallback
content.source: Migrated from bead argus-o2m.3; parent/source bead argus-o2m. Reference user stories: US12, US13, US14, US15, US16, US17.
content.what_to_build: Make launch-time restore conditional on the spec restore policy and verify invalid snapshots fall back to a fresh session without crashing. Restore must be skipped when disabled by environment, when running under automated tests, or when launched with file/URL arguments. Runtime-only state must stay out of snapshots and restored sessions.

dependencies.title: Phase 4: Round-trip a one-window session skeleton

dependents.title: Phase 4: Verify full-session behavior across save, quit, and relaunch

title: Phase 4: Enforce launch restore policy and safe fallback

acceptance_criteria.text: Terminal panel snapshots include scrollback text capped at 400,000 characters per panel.
Truncation never starts replay inside a partial ANSI escape sequence.
Truncated scrollback is wrapped with ANSI reset codes so colors/styles do not leak incorrectly into restored content.
Restored terminal panels show the saved scrollback before or alongside the new shell in the intended Phase 4 behavior.
Pure tests cover plain text, empty scrollback, exact-limit content, over-limit content, complete ANSI sequences, and partial ANSI sequences at the truncation boundary.
Integration tests verify a workspace save/restore includes terminal scrollback for the right terminal panel.

agent_notes: Blocked by migrated issue for argus-o2m.2.

There is no scrollback capture API in the current wrapper. Prefer a deep, testable scrollback formatter/truncator module with a tiny Ghostty integration seam. Validate GhosttyKit capabilities before choosing capture/replay; keep failures safe and do not block quit indefinitely.

files.reason: Terminal surface state and Ghostty integration seam.

content.goal: Phase 4: Capture and replay terminal scrollback safely
content.source: Migrated from bead argus-o2m.4; parent/source bead argus-o2m. Reference user stories: US6, US7, US10, US11.
content.what_to_build: Add terminal scrollback persistence for terminal panels. Capture up to 400,000 characters per terminal panel, truncate without replaying from the middle of an ANSI escape sequence, wrap truncated replay text in ANSI reset codes, and restore that scrollback into reopened terminal panels without corrupting terminal display state.

title: Phase 4: Capture and replay terminal scrollback safely

acceptance_criteria.text: Panel snapshots are typed and can round-trip terminal and browser panel payloads without ambiguity.
Browser panel snapshots include URL, zoom level, and developer tools visibility.
Unknown or unavailable browser panel restoration is safe and deterministic if browser UI support is not present yet.
Tests cover Codable round trip for browser panel state and mixed terminal/browser panel ordering.
The design does not require implementing the WKWebView browser UI as part of this slice.

agent_notes: Blocked by migrated issue for argus-o2m.2.
This task is about the persistence contract and restoration extension point, not Phase 5 browser UI. Keep the schema durable and product-oriented: URL, zoom, developer tools visibility.

files.reason: No BrowserPanel implementation exists yet; this task defines persistence contract only.

content.goal: Phase 4: Persist typed browser panel state contract
content.source: Migrated from bead argus-o2m.5; parent/source bead argus-o2m. Reference user stories: US8.
content.what_to_build: Add the Phase 4 persistence contract for browser panels even if the full Browser Panels feature is implemented later. The session format should support typed browser panel snapshots with current URL, page zoom level, and developer tools visibility, and restoration should be routed through the same panel snapshot mechanism used by terminal panels.

title: Phase 4: Persist typed browser panel state contract

acceptance_criteria.text: Mutations to workspace, panel, project, sidebar, and restorable panel state mark the session dirty.
Autosave writes after an approximately 8-second quiet interval and coalesces rapid changes into a single save.
Autosave does not block the main thread with file I/O during normal operation.
applicationWillTerminate performs a synchronous save before termination continues.
Autosave tests use a controllable scheduler/clock or equivalent seam rather than slow wall-clock sleeps.
Save failures are reported/logged without crashing normal UI flows; quit-save behavior remains deterministic.

agent_notes: Blocked by migrated issue for argus-o2m.1.
Centralize save scheduling likely outside WorkspaceManager, while keeping UI state mutations on the main thread and disk I/O off the main thread except required quit save.

files.reason: Current synchronous lifecycle hook calls WorkspaceManager.saveSession().
Current persistence entry point to replace/centralize.

content.goal: Phase 4: Autosave quiet sessions and save synchronously on quit
content.source: Migrated from bead argus-o2m.6; parent/source bead argus-o2m. Reference user stories: US10, US11.
content.what_to_build: Add a save policy that persists the full session automatically about every 8 seconds during typing-quiet periods and writes synchronously during application termination. The policy should avoid excessive writes during high-volume terminal activity while ensuring normal quit completes only after the session is durably written.

dependencies.title: Phase 4: Round-trip a one-window session skeleton

dependents.title: Phase 4: Verify full-session behavior across save, quit, and relaunch

title: Phase 4: Autosave quiet sessions and save synchronously on quit

acceptance_criteria.text: A rich session containing projects, catch-all workspace, multiple workspaces, multiple tabs, splits, sidebar state, selected workspace, selected panel, terminal directories, and scrollback restores correctly.
Browser panel state snapshots are included in verification through the typed persistence contract or available browser implementation.
Restore suppression cases still produce a fresh session in the end-to-end path.
Ephemeral runtime state is not present after restore.
The test suite documents Phase 4 prior art so future Phase 5/6 work can extend persistence without re-opening core session behavior.

agent_notes: Blocked by migrated issues for argus-o2m.3, argus-o2m.4, argus-o2m.5, and argus-o2m.6.
Prefer deterministic unit/integration tests over brittle UI automation. Test behavior users care about, not incidental private representations.

files.reason: Add Phase 4 tests near the persistence feature and reuse existing helpers/patterns from Phase 2 and Phase 3.

content.goal: Phase 4: Verify full-session behavior across save, quit, and relaunch
content.source: Migrated from bead argus-o2m.7; parent/source bead argus-o2m. Reference user stories: US1 through US17.
content.what_to_build: Add final Phase 4 verification coverage that exercises the complete persistence feature as a user would experience it: create or load a rich session, save it, simulate quit/relaunch, and verify the restored app state while confirming unsafe or ephemeral state is absent.
